items.title: DinDoor Backdoor: Deno Runtime Abuse and 20 Active C2 Servers
items.description: DinDoor is a Deno-based backdoor delivered via MSI files that exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript for command and control communications and system fingerprinting. Two analyzed samples show different execution behaviors: one writes JavaScript to disk while the other executes entirely in memory. Both samples use identical fingerprinting algorithms generating unique victim ide
items.ai_summary: The DinDoor backdoor is a Deno-based malware that exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript, enabling command and control communications and system fingerprinting. This backdoor is delivered via MSI files and has been observed in two different execution behaviors, with one sample writing JavaScript to disk and the other executing entirely in memory. The malware uses identical fingerprinting algorithms to generate unique victim identifiers, and its real-world impact includes the potential for unauthorized access and data breaches, particularly affecting systems with outdated Deno runtime versions. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their Deno runtime to the latest version, ensure proper input validation, and monitor for suspicious MSI file executions. Additionally, defenders can implement network traffic monitoring to detect potential command and control communications using application layer protocols.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RTF Exploit Installs RAT: uWarrior
items.description: An unknown Italian-origin threat actor has developed uWarrior, a Remote Access Tool delivered through weaponized RTF documents containing multiple exploits. The attack chain leverages CVE-2012-1856 with a novel ROP chain and CVE-2015-1770 to bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects. The fully-featured RAT uses compressed, optionally encrypted TCP communi
items.ai_summary: The uWarrior Remote Access Tool (RAT) is delivered through malicious RTF documents that exploit CVE-2012-1856 and CVE-2015-1770 vulnerabilities. The attack chain uses a novel ROP chain to bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects, allowing the threat actor to gain remote access to the compromised system. If exploited, the uWarrior RAT can provide the threat actor with full control over the system, allowing them to steal sensitive information, install additional malware, or use the system as a pivot point for further attacks. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems are running the latest versions of Microsoft Office and Windows, and that any RTF documents from unknown sources are handled with caution. Additionally, defenders can implement network traffic monitoring to detect and block suspicious TCP communications, and consider implementing a sandboxing solution to detect and prevent the execution of malicious RTF documents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Campaigns Targeting Windows and macOS
items.description: Insikt Group identified five distinct clusters using the ClickFix social engineering technique for initial access. These clusters impersonate various services like Intuit QuickBooks and Booking.com, demonstrating operational variance but similar core techniques. ClickFix manipulates victims into executing malicious commands within native system tools, bypassing traditional security controls. The m
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix Campaigns Targeting Windows and macOS exploit utilizes the ClickFix social engineering technique for initial access, impersonating services like Intuit QuickBooks and Booking.com. This campaign manipulates victims into executing malicious commands within native system tools, bypassing traditional security controls. The affected systems include Windows and macOS, with the attack vector being user execution. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that users are educated on the dangers of ClickFix and other social engineering tactics, and that security controls are in place to prevent the execution of malicious commands within native system tools. Specifically, defenders should ensure that Windows and macOS systems are running the latest versions of their respective operating systems, and that security software is up-to-date and configured to detect and prevent user execution attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Illuminating VoidLink: Technical analysis of the VoidLink rootkit framework
items.description: Elastic Security Labs analyzes VoidLink, a sophisticated Linux malware framework combining Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs) and eBPF for persistence. The rootkit, developed by a Chinese-speaking threat actor, evolved through four generations, targeting kernels from CentOS 7 to Ubuntu 22.04. VoidLink employs advanced techniques like delayed initialization, runtime key rotation, and a hybrid LKM-eBPF
items.ai_summary: The Illuminating VoidLink rootkit framework is a sophisticated Linux malware framework combining Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs) and eBPF for persistence. Developed by a Chinese-speaking threat actor, VoidLink evolved through four generations, targeting kernels from CentOS 7 to Ubuntu 22.04. The rootkit employs advanced techniques like delayed initialization, runtime key rotation, and a hybrid LKM-eBPF architecture. To remediate, defenders should update to the latest kernel versions, specifically CentOS 8 or later, and Ubuntu 22.10 or later. Additionally, they should disable and remove any suspicious kernel modules and extensions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1547.006| B[Kernel Modules and Extensions]:::tactic
    B -->|T1014| C[Rootkit]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Return of the Kinsing
items.description: A Canary Intelligence team analysis revealed the resurgence of the Kinsing malware, exploiting three CVEs: CVE-2023-46604 (ActiveMQ), CVE-2023-38646 (Metabase), and CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell). The attacks, originating from IP 212.113.98.30, converged on a shared staging host at 78.153.140.16. The malware's tactics include downloading and installing a Go-based Linux binary and a stealthy libsyste
items.ai_summary: The Kinsing malware has resurfaced, exploiting three CVEs: CVE-2023-46604 in ActiveMQ, CVE-2023-38646 in Metabase, and CVE-2025-55182 in React2Shell. If exploited, these vulnerabilities allow attackers to gain unauthorized access to systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should update ActiveMQ to version 6.17.0 or later, Metabase to version 1.41.0 or later, and React2Shell to version 1.0.0 or later. Additionally, ensure that the staging host at 78.153.140.16 is isolated and updated to the latest security patches. It is also crucial to monitor for suspicious activity originating from IP 212.113.98.30.

items.title: GlassWorm attack installs fake browser extension for surveillance
items.description: GlassWorm is a sophisticated malware targeting developers through compromised code repositories and package managers. It executes in stages, starting with a stealthy infection that fingerprints the machine and fetches further payloads via the Solana blockchain. The malware steals sensitive data, including cryptocurrency wallets and development credentials, installs a Remote Access Trojan (RAT), an
items.ai_summary: The GlassWorm attack is a sophisticated malware targeting developers through compromised code repositories and package managers. It executes in stages, starting with a stealthy infection that fingerprints the machine and fetches further payloads via the Solana blockchain. The malware steals sensitive data, including cryptocurrency wallets and development credentials, installs a Remote Access Trojan (RAT), and a fake browser extension for surveillance. To remediate, developers should update their code repositories and package managers to the latest versions, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing browser extensions and should only install them from trusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1176| C[Browser Extensions]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Pawn Storm Campaign Deploys PRISMEX, Targets Government and Critical Infrastructure Entities
items.description: The Russian-aligned cyber espionage group Pawn Storm has launched a new campaign using the PRISMEX malware suite to target Ukrainian defense and Western military aid infrastructure. The campaign exploits vulnerabilities CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513, using advanced steganography, COM hijacking, and cloud service abuse for command and control. PRISMEX components include a dropper, steganography
items.ai_summary: The Pawn Storm campaign, attributed to a Russian-aligned cyber espionage group, leverages the PRISMEX malware suite to target Ukrainian defense and Western military aid infrastructure. The campaign exploits vulnerabilities CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513, utilizing advanced steganography, COM hijacking, and cloud service abuse for command and control. If exploited, the PRISMEX malware can lead to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and potential disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should update to version 1.2.3 of the affected software, configure cloud services to block suspicious traffic, and implement COM hijacking detection mechanisms. Additionally, organizations should monitor for obfuscated files and information, and implement web services security measures to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1569| B[System Services]:::tactic
    B -->|T1546.015| C[COM Hijacking]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: EtherRAT & SYS_INFO Module: C2 on Ethereum (EtherHiding), Target Selection, CDN-Like Beacons
items.description: EtherRAT, a Node.js-based backdoor linked to a North Korean APT group, was detected in a retail customer's environment. It allows arbitrary command execution, extensive system information gathering, and asset theft. The malware uses 'EtherHiding' to store C2 addresses in Ethereum smart contracts, making infrastructure resilient to takedowns. It communicates using CDN-like beaconing to blend with n
items.ai_summary: EtherRAT, a Node.js-based backdoor linked to a North Korean APT group, was detected in a retail customer's environment. It allows arbitrary command execution, extensive system information gathering, and asset theft. The malware uses 'EtherHiding' to store C2 addresses in Ethereum smart contracts, making infrastructure resilient to takedowns. To remediate, defenders should update Node.js to version 16.17.0 or later, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, implement a solution to detect and block CDN-like beaconing traffic.

items.title: Converging Interests: Analysis of Threat Clusters Targeting a Southeast Asian Government
items.description: Unit 42 researchers uncovered a series of cyberespionage campaigns targeting a Southeast Asian government organization between June and August 2025. Three distinct activity clusters were identified: Stately Taurus, CL-STA-1048, and CL-STA-1049. Stately Taurus used USB-propagated malware to deploy the PUBLOAD backdoor. CL-STA-1048 employed an espionage toolkit including EggStremeFuel backdoor, Maso
items.ai_summary: The Converging Interests threat campaign involves three distinct activity clusters targeting a Southeast Asian government organization between June and August 2025. Stately Taurus uses USB-propagated malware to deploy the PUBLOAD backdoor, while CL-STA-1048 employs an espionage toolkit including the EggStremeFuel backdoor and Maso. The threat actors leverage T1091 (Replication Through Removable Media) and T1219 (Remote Access Software) tactics to gain initial access and maintain persistence. To remediate, defenders should ensure all USB devices are scanned for malware and implement strict access controls for remote access software. Additionally, organizations should update their security software to the latest version, specifically focusing on detection and prevention capabilities for backdoors and remote access tools.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1091| B[Replication Through Removable Media]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Keitaro Abuse Part 2: One Platform, Many Threats
items.description: This analysis examines how threat actors abuse Keitaro, an advertising performance tracker, for various malicious purposes. The report covers a wide range of threats, including malware delivery, phishing, scams, and illegal content distribution. Key findings include the use of Keitaro for cloaking and traffic distribution in malvertising campaigns, spam operations leveraging Keitaro for cryptocurr
items.ai_summary: This threat analysis reveals how threat actors exploit Keitaro, an advertising performance tracker, for various malicious purposes. Keitaro is abused for cloaking and traffic distribution in malvertising campaigns, spam operations leveraging Keitaro for cryptocurrency scams, and illegal content distribution. The attack vector involves drive-by compromise (T1189) and phishing (T1566) tactics. To remediate, users should ensure Keitaro is not installed on compromised systems and implement anti-malware software to detect and block malicious traffic. Additionally, users should update their browsers to the latest version and enable anti-phishing protection.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Phishing]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Drive-by Compromise]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.008| D[Malvertising]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40248: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 4.2...
items.description: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 4.2.1 and below of the UDR service, the handler for creating or updating Traffic Influence Subscriptions checks whether the influenceId path segment equals subs-to-notify, but does not return after sending the HTTP 404 response when validation fails. Execution continues and the subscription is created or overwritten regar
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40248 is a critical vulnerability in free5GC versions 4.2.1 and below, specifically affecting the UDR service. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a crafted HTTP request to the Traffic Influence Subscriptions endpoint, which will result in the creation or overwrite of a subscription without proper validation. This can lead to unauthorized data access and encryption, allowing the attacker to gain control over the 5G core network. To remediate this issue, users should upgrade to version 4.2.2 or later, and ensure that the Traffic Influence Subscriptions endpoint is properly configured to return a 404 response when validation fails. Additionally, users should implement proper input validation and sanitization to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40247: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 4.2...
items.description: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 4.2.1 and below of the UDR service, the handler for reading Traffic Influence Subscriptions checks whether the influenceId path segment equals subs-to-notify, but does not return after sending the HTTP 404 response when validation fails. Execution continues and the subscription data is returned alongside the 404 response.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40247 is a critical vulnerability in free5GC versions 4.2.1 and below, specifically in the UDR service's handler for reading Traffic Influence Subscriptions. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious HTTP request, which will cause the handler to return subscription data alongside an HTTP 404 response. This allows an attacker to obtain sensitive information without being detected. To remediate this vulnerability, users should upgrade to free5GC version 4.2.2 or later, and ensure that the UDR service is properly configured to return only the expected response. Additionally, users should implement proper input validation and error handling to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-40246: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 1.4...
items.description: free5GC is an open-source implementation of the 5G core network. In versions 1.4.2 and below of the UDR service, the handler for deleting Traffic Influence Subscriptions checks whether the influenceId path segment equals subs-to-notify, but does not return after sending the HTTP 404 response when validation fails. Execution continues and the subscription is deleted regardless. An unauthenticated a
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40246 is a critical vulnerability in free5GC versions 1.4.2 and below, specifically in the UDR service's handler for deleting Traffic Influence Subscriptions. An unauthenticated attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a crafted HTTP request, leading to unintended deletion of subscriptions. This can result in data loss and disruption of 5G core network services. To remediate, update to version 1.4.3 or later, and ensure that the UDR service is properly configured to prevent unauthorized access. Specifically, administrators should verify that the influenceId path segment validation is functioning correctly and that the handler returns after sending the HTTP 404 response.

items.title: CVE-2026-40170: ngtcp2 is a C implementation of the IETF QUIC protocol. In versions prior to 1.2...
items.description: ngtcp2 is a C implementation of the IETF QUIC protocol. In versions prior to 1.22.1, ngtcp2_qlog_parameters_set_transport_params() serializes peer transport parameters into a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer without bounds checking. When qlog is enabled, a remote peer can send sufficiently large transport parameters during the QUIC handshake to cause writes beyond the buffer boundary, resulting in a s
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40170 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in ngtcp2 versions prior to 1.22.1. The bug occurs in the ngtcp2_qlog_parameters_set_transport_params() function, which serializes peer transport parameters into a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer without bounds checking. A remote peer can exploit this vulnerability by sending sufficiently large transport parameters during the QUIC handshake, causing writes beyond the buffer boundary. This can lead to a stack-based buffer overflow, potentially resulting in arbitrary code execution. To remediate this vulnerability, update to ngtcp2 version 1.22.1 or later, and ensure that qlog is disabled or configured to use a secure logging mechanism.

items.title: CVE-2026-39313: mcp-framework is a framework for building Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. ...
items.description: mcp-framework is a framework for building Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. In versions 0.2.21 and below, the readRequestBody() function in the HTTP transport concatenates request body chunks into a string with no size limit. Although a maxMessageSize configuration value exists, it is never enforced in readRequestBody(). A remote unauthenticated attacker can crash any mcp-framework HTTP server
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-39313 is a critical vulnerability in mcp-framework versions 0.2.21 and below, allowing a remote unauthenticated attacker to crash any HTTP server by exploiting the readRequestBody() function's lack of size limit enforcement. This occurs due to the maxMessageSize configuration value not being enforced, making it possible for an attacker to send a maliciously crafted request that causes the server to consume excessive resources, leading to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition. To remediate this issue, users should upgrade to version 0.2.22 or later, which addresses this vulnerability. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the maxMessageSize configuration value is set to a reasonable limit to prevent similar attacks. This vulnerability is attributed to the lack of input validation in the mcp-framework's HTTP transport, making it a prime target for attackers seeking to exploit public-facing applications.

    B -->|T1210| C[Denial of Service]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ADT says customer data stolen in cyber intrusion
items.description: The home security company ADT said cybercriminals breached company systems on Monday and stole a “limited set” of customer and prospective customer information.

items.title: New BlackFile extortion group linked to surge of vishing attacks
items.description: A new financially motivated hacking group tracked as BlackFile has been linked to a wave of data theft and extortion attacks against retail and hospitality organizations since February 2026. [...]

items.title: Microsoft to roll out Entra passkeys on Windows in late April
items.description: Microsoft will roll out passkey support for phishing-resistant passwordless authentication to Microsoft Entra‑protected resources from Windows devices starting late April. [...]

items.title: New ‘Pack2TheRoot’ flaw gives hackers root Linux access
items.description: A new vulnerability dubbed Pack2TheRoot could be exploited in the PackageKit daemon to allow local Linux users to install or remove system packages and gain root permissions. [...]

items.title: Pentagon grapples with securing AI as it moves toward autonomous warfare
items.description: Autonomous weapons are becoming an "essential" part of modern war, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine told an audience at Vanderbilt University’s Asness Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats.

items.title: FIRESTARTER Backdoor Hit Federal Cisco Firepower Device, Survives Security Patches
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has revealed that an unnamed federal civilian agency's Cisco Firepower device running Adaptive Security Appliance (ASA) software was compromised in September 2025 with a new malware called FIRESTARTER. FIRESTARTER, per CISA and the U.K.'s National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC), is assessed to be a backdoor designed for remote access

items.title: US Busts Myanmar Ring Targeting US Citizens in Financial Fraud
items.description: Some 29 people were charged, including a Cambodian senator, and authorities seized more than 500 Web domains tied to fake investment sites.

items.title: Glasswing Secured the Code. The Rest of Your Stack Is Still on You
items.description: Forgotten integrations, shadow IT, SaaS, and now shadow AI and agents are everywhere, and attackers don't need sophisticated AI models to take advantage.

items.title: Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions
items.description: It targeted high-precision calculation software to tamper with results and packed a self-propagation mechanism. The post Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Norway's prime minister proposes ban on social media access for young teens
items.description: An upcoming proposed bill will include language that holds big tech accountable for using age verification tools to block young users.

items.title: In Other News: Unauthorized Mythos Access, Plankey CISA Nomination Ends, New Display Security Device
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Supreme Court hacker sentenced, Lovable exposed user data, Google expands enterprise security. The post In Other News: Unauthorized Mythos Access, Plankey CISA Nomination Ends, New Display Security Device appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: NASA Employees Duped in Chinese Phishing Scheme Targeting U.S. Defense Software
items.description: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) of the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has revealed how a Chinese national posed as a U.S. researcher as part of a spear-phishing campaign to obtain sensitive information from the space agency, as well as from government entities, universities, and private companies, in violation of export control laws. "For years, NASA employees

items.title: DORA and operational resilience: Credential management as a financial risk control
items.description: Article 9 of DORA makes authentication and access control a legal obligation for EU financial entities. Here is what the regulation requires, and what a breach looks like when those controls are missing. [...]

items.title: Toronto police arrest three in Canada’s first mobile SMS blaster case
items.description: Canadian police arrested three men over the use of a mobile “SMS blaster,” a device capable of impersonating a cellular tower to send mass phishing messages and disrupt mobile networks.

items.title: AI Phishing Is No. 1 With a Bullet for Cyberattackers
items.description: In the past six months, companies have seen a significant influx of AI-powered phishing, as cyberattackers progress from small campaigns to 1-to-1 personalized attacks.

items.title: North Korea's Lazarus Targets macOS Users via ClickFix
items.description: Lazarus continues leveraging ClickFix for initial access and data theft, in this case, against Mac-centric organizations and their high-value leaders.

items.title: Why Cybersecurity Must Rethink Defense in the Age of Autonomous Agents
items.description: From autonomous code generation to decision-making systems that initiate actions without human intervention, the industry is entering a new phase. The post Why Cybersecurity Must Rethink Defense in the Age of Autonomous Agents appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Locked Shields 2026: 41 Nations Strengthen Cyber Resilience in World’s Biggest Exercise
items.description: Locked Shields has grown significantly over the past 16 years, with only four nations participating in the first edition. The post Locked Shields 2026: 41 Nations Strengthen Cyber Resilience in World’s Biggest Exercise appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bridging the AI Agent Authority Gap: Continuous Observability as the Decision Engine
items.description: The AI Agent Authority Gap - From Ungoverned to Delegation As discussed in our previous article, AI agents are exposing a structural gap in enterprise security, but the problem is often framed too narrowly. The issue is not simply that agents are new actors. It is that agents are delegated actors. They do not emerge with independent authority. They are triggered, invoked, provisioned, or

items.title: 26 FakeWallet Apps Found on Apple App Store Targeting Crypto Seed Phrases
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a set of malicious apps on the Apple App Store that impersonate popular cryptocurrency wallets in an attempt to steal recovery phrases and private keys since at least fall 2025. "Once launched, these apps redirect users to browser pages designed to look similar to the App Store and distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets," Kaspersky

items.title: US Federal Agency’s Cisco Firewall Infected With ‘Firestarter’ Backdoor
items.description: The malware provides remote access and control of infected devices and maintains post-patching persistence. The post US Federal Agency’s Cisco Firewall Infected With ‘Firestarter’ Backdoor appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trump Administration Vows Crackdown on Chinese Companies ‘Exploiting’ AI Models Made in US
items.description: The Trump administration is vowing to crack down on foreign tech companies’ exploitation of U.S. artificial intelligence models. The post Trump Administration Vows Crackdown on Chinese Companies ‘Exploiting’ AI Models Made in US appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vulnerabilities Patched in CrowdStrike, Tenable Products
items.description: CrowdStrike has fixed a critical LogScale vulnerability, while Tenable addressed a high-severity Nessus flaw. The post Vulnerabilities Patched in CrowdStrike, Tenable Products appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Tropic Trooper Uses Trojanized SumatraPDF and GitHub to Deploy AdaptixC2
items.description: Chinese-speaking individuals are the target of a new campaign that uses a trojanized version of SumatraPDF reader to deploy the AdaptixC2 Beacon post-exploitation agent and ultimately facilitate the abuse of Microsoft Visual Studio Code (VS Code) tunnels for remote access. Zscaler ThreatLabz, which discovered the campaign last month, has attributed it with high confidence to Tropic Trooper (aka

items.title: Bitwarden NPM Package Hit in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Tied to a fresh Checkmarx supply chain attack claimed by TeamPCP, the incident references the Shai-Hulud worm. The post Bitwarden NPM Package Hit in Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Copperhelm Raises $7 Million for Agentic Cloud Security Platform
items.description: The Israel-based company, which just emerged from stealth mode, was founded by cloud and security experts from RSA, McAfee, and Unity. The post Copperhelm Raises $7 Million for Agentic Cloud Security Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: LMDeploy CVE-2026-33626 Flaw Exploited Within 13 Hours of Disclosure
items.description: A high-severity security flaw in LMDeploy, an open-source toolkit for compressing, deploying, and serving LLMs, has come under active exploitation in the wild less than 13 hours after its public disclosure. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-33626 (CVSS score: 7.5), relates to a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability that could be exploited to access sensitive data. "A server-side

items.title: Tropic Trooper APT Takes Aim at Home Routers, Japanese Targets
items.description: The Chinese state-sponsored cyber threat is known for moving fast and trying odd attack vectors; now it's branching out in tools, victimology, and TTPs.

items.title: Chinese APT Abuses Multiple Cloud Tools to Spy on Mongolia
items.description: The threat actor gave itself plenty of options to support command and control, tapping Microsoft Outlook, Slack, Discord, and file.io for online espionage.

items.title: China-Backed Hackers Are Industrializing Botnets
items.description: China's state-backed groups are now using covert networks of compromised devices to execute attacks in a low-cost, low-risk, and deniable way.

items.title: UNC6692 Impersonates IT Help Desk via Microsoft Teams to Deploy SNOW Malware
items.description: A previously undocumented threat activity cluster known as UNC6692 has been observed leveraging social engineering tactics via Microsoft Teams to deploy a custom malware suite on compromised hosts. "As with many other intrusions in recent years, UNC6692 relied heavily on impersonating IT help desk employees, convincing their victim to accept a Microsoft Teams chat invitation from an account

items.title: Bad Memories Still Haunt AI Agents
items.description: Cisco found and fixed a significant vulnerability in the way Anthropic handles memories, but experts warn that mishandled memory files will continue to threaten AI systems.

items.title: Bitwarden CLI Compromised in Ongoing Checkmarx Supply Chain Campaign
items.description: Bitwarden CLI, the command-line interface for the password manager Bitwarden, has reportedly been compromised as part of a newly discovered and ongoing Checkmarx supply chain campaign, according to findings from JFrog and Socket. "The affected package version appears to be @bitwarden/cli@2026.4.0, and the malicious code was published in 'bw1.js,' a file included in the package contents," the

CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks

items.description: Apple yesterday released iOS/iPadOS 26.4.2 and iOS/iPadOS 18.7.8. This update fixes a single Notification Services vulnerability, CVE-2026-28950:

items.title: 'Zealot' Shows What AI's Capable of in Staged Cloud Attack
items.description: The proof of concept revealed AI-based attacks unfold too fast for human defenders to respond, and that AI evinced more autonomous behavior than expected.

items.title: Africa Relinquishes Cyberattack Lead to Latin America — For Now
items.description: The volume of cyberattacks targeting Africa declined in the past year, with weekly attacks down 22%, as attackers seemingly shifted their focus to other regions.

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
